title: The Memory Limit of One GPU
concept: An LLM with 70 billion parameters requires 140GB of memory just to store weights, exceeding the memory of any single GPU card.

exercises.prompt: What is true about the size of large language models (weights) parameters of large language models)?

options.text: They are very small text files.
They require massive amounts of memory.
They do not use any memory.

exercises.explain: Modern LLMs have billions of parameters, making their file size and memory requirements extremely large.

cards.title: Too Big for One Screen
cards.body: Modern AI models are getting smarter, but they are also getting incredibly massive. Have you ever wondered why we can't just run the latest ultra-smart AI chatbots on a single high-end computer? It turns out, they are literally too big to fit!

cards.title: What is the GPU Memory Limit?
cards.body: AI models store what they learn as numbers called parameters (or weights). A massive 70-billion-parameter LLM needs at least 2 bytes of memory per parameter. This means it requires 140 gigabytes of memory just to load, which is far more than the memory limit of any single GPU card.

cards.title: The Backpack Squeeze
cards.body: Imagine trying to fit a giant collection of 70 thick textbooks into a backpack that can only hold 40. No matter how neatly you pack them, the books simply physical space limitations. The giant AI model is like those textbooks, and the GPU is the backpack.

cards.title: The 70B Parameter Wall
cards.body: A popular open-source AI like Llama-2-70B has 70 billion parameters. To run it, you need 140GB of memory. However, a top-tier professional GPU like the Nvidia A100 only has up to 80GB of memory. Trying to load the model on a single card results in an 'Out of Memory' error.

cards.title: Myth: The Ultimate GPU Can Do It All
cards.body: People often think that if you buy the most expensive, top-of-the-line GPU in the world, it can run any AI model by itself. In reality, even the most powerful single GPUs max out at 80GB or 140GB of memory, which is still too small to hold today's largest models.

cards.title: Size Comparison
cards.body: Comparing the memory size of a single high-end GPU with the memory needed by a large 70B AI model.

left.text: Has up to 80GB of memory capacity.

right.text: Requires 140GB of memory to load weights.

exercises.prompt: What happens when you try to load a 70-billion-parameter AI model onto one single GPU?

options.text: It is way too small and leaves most GPU memory empty.
It exceeds the memory capacity of any single GPU card.
It requires special cooling that only CPU chips can provide.
It fits perfectly on any standard laptop graphics card.

exercises.explain: A single high-end GPU card typically has around 80GB of memory, while a 70B parameter model requires 140GB, making it too large to fit on one card.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A 70-billion-parameter LLM is too large to fit into the memory of a single standard GPU.
exercises.explain: Even though a single GPU is incredibly fast, it lacks the physical memory capacity (usually maxing out at 80GB) to hold a 140GB model.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the memory requirement for a 70B parameter model.
exercises.sentence: A 70-billion-parameter LLM requires at least 140GB of memory just to store its weights.
exercises.explain: A 70 billion parameter model requires 2 bytes per parameter, meaning it needs 140GB of memory just to store its weights, which is more than a single GPU can hold.

exercises.prompt: Which of these scenarios represents the GPU memory limit problem?

options.text: A single GPU running a 70B parameter model without any issues.
An engineer getting an 'Out of Memory' error when loading a 70B model on one GPU.
A small 7B model loading completely fine on a single 80GB GPU.

exercises.explain: The 70B model requires 140GB, which exceeds a single 80GB GPU. A 7B model requires only 14GB, which easily fits. Using multiple GPUs is the correct way to run the 70B model.

exercises.prompt: Why is it physically impossible to load a standard 70-billion-parameter LLM onto a single modern GPU card for training or inference?

options.text: Because a single GPU does not have enough computing cores to run math operations.
Because the model's weights alone require 140GB, exceeding the memory limit of any single GPU.
Because massive AI models can only run on CPU memory, not GPU memory.
Because GPUs are programmed to refuse models that have more than 10 billion parameters.

exercises.explain: An AI model with 70 billion parameters needs about 140 gigabytes of space just to exist in memory, which easily overflows the memory capacity of any single commercial GPU card (which usually top out at 80 gigabytes).
